findings.claim: WordPress 6.2 introduced the 'css' property in theme.json, allowing raw CSS strings to be attached globally, per-block, and per-element without external stylesheets
WordPress 6.6 expanded the css property to support elements (links, headings, buttons) and style variations, and introduced theme.json version 3 as the recommended schema

The '&' selector in theme.json css property references the block's own container, enabling pseudo-classes (&:hover, &:focus), pseudo-elements (&::before, &::after), and child selectors (&  h2) without knowing the generated class name

Gradient text is implemented in block themes via theme.json css property using background-clip: text combined with linear-gradient and -webkit-text-fill-color: transparent on core/heading blocks -- no wp:html block needed

Glassmorphism (backdrop-filter: blur) is implemented as a Block Style Variation on core/group or core/cover blocks, with the CSS applied via theme.json styles.blocks.variations or the css property -- no native UI control exists for backdrop-filter
WordPress 6.6 introduced Section Styles (Extended Block Style Variations) allowing a variation on a container block to automatically re-theme all nested child blocks, headings, buttons, and elements

WordPress 6.6 standardized all generated block/global styles to uniform 0-1-0 specificity using :root :where() wrappers, making section style overrides work via source order instead of selector weight
register_block_style() (since WP 5.3) adds is-style-{name} classes to blocks; can include inline_style for small CSS or style_handle for enqueued stylesheets
WordPress 6.6+ allows block style variations to be registered via standalone JSON files in the /styles directory using a blockTypes array, requiring zero PHP
wp_enqueue_block_style() conditionally loads CSS only when the specific block is present on the page; files under ~20KB are automatically inlined into the document head

Premium block themes (Ollie, Frost, Wabi) use a modular assets/styles/blocks/ directory with per-block CSS files (core-group.css, core-navigation.css) loaded via wp_enqueue_block_style for performance

Twenty Twenty-Four theme uses minimal style.css (metadata only), defines all design tokens in theme.json, and loads block-specific CSS via wp_enqueue_block_style() with register_block_style() for variations like 'Pill' and 'Outline'
Twenty Twenty-Four includes style variations as separate JSON files in /styles directory (onyx.json, ember.json) providing complete alternate color palettes and typography sets

WordPress.org theme review guidelines have no explicit ban on wp:html blocks, but themes are rejected if wp:html contains hardcoded text (violates i18n requirement) or recreates layouts achievable with core blocks

The 'Core Block First' review guideline means themes MUST use core blocks (wp:group, wp:columns, wp:heading) when they can achieve the same layout -- wp:html is only acceptable for complex SVGs or purely decorative non-text elements

Block themes can load complex layouts as PHP-based Block Patterns (in /patterns directory) which support translation functions and can wrap core blocks with specific layout settings (constrained, wide) without custom CSS classes
CSS Custom Properties generated from theme.json follow the pattern --wp--preset--{category}--{slug} (e.g., --wp--preset--color--primary, --wp--preset--spacing--20) and are available globally
Fluid typography and spacing use clamp() directly in theme.json settings, eliminating the need for media queries in block theme stylesheets
WordPress 6.7 introduced automatic class naming for Block Variations (not Style Variations) in the format wp-block-{blockName}-{variationName}
theme.json css property output is rendered in the document head within a <style> block identified as global-styles-inline-css, loaded for both frontend and editor
CSS defined in theme.json has lower specificity than user-added CSS in the Site Editor, ensuring user customizations always win over theme defaults

In block themes, style.css is automatically enqueued for both frontend and editor (unlike classic themes where manual enqueue was required), but it serves primarily as metadata header with minimal CSS
Box shadow support was added natively to the Block Editor UI for Group, Stack, and Row blocks in WordPress 6.7, reducing the need for custom CSS for depth effects
Media queries (@media) are fully supported within theme.json css property strings, enabling responsive overrides without external stylesheets

key_insights: Premium block themes achieve all advanced CSS effects (gradient text, glassmorphism, CSS grid, backdrop-filter) through THREE layered mechanisms -- never wp:html: (1) theme.json css property for per-block raw CSS with & selector, (2) Block Style Variations registered via PHP register_block_style() or JSON files in /styles, and (3) wp_enqueue_block_style() for conditionally-loaded external CSS files. This is confirmed by WordPress.org review guidelines which mandate core blocks over wp:html.

WordPress 6.6 was the pivotal release: it introduced Section Styles (variations that cascade to nested child blocks), uniform 0-1-0 CSS specificity via :root :where() wrappers, theme.json v3, and JSON-file-based style registration in /styles -- collectively making it possible to build complex 'colorway' and 'section' designs entirely declaratively without PHP.

The performance architecture of premium block themes (Ollie, Frost, Twenty Twenty-Four) follows a strict pattern: design tokens in theme.json generate CSS custom properties, block-specific CSS lives in modular files loaded conditionally via wp_enqueue_block_style() (auto-inlined if under ~20KB), and style.css contains only the required metadata header -- resulting in minimal initial CSS payload and improved Core Web Vitals.
gaps: No authoritative benchmarks found comparing CSS payload size between theme.json-only themes vs themes using wp_enqueue_block_style for complex designs
Limited documentation on how the WordPress KSES filter interacts with advanced CSS properties (backdrop-filter, background-clip) in wp:html blocks vs theme.json css property
No clear guidance on maximum recommended size or complexity for theme.json css property strings before external stylesheets become preferable
Sparse information on how the Interactivity API (WP 6.5) interplays with CSS architecture for animated or stateful visual effects in block themes
Could not find official WordPress.org statistics on what percentage of approved block themes use wp:html vs pure core blocks
